speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. Stephanie, you were saying that Vera and the children were able to move to the sanatorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And under what permission-- under what circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under a doctor's certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under a doctor's certificate. And how was that doctor's certificate obtained?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Jewish hospitals. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So someone from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Jewish hospital department.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. And they were--and then they were allowed to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And because it was a Jewish institution, the-- the Judenrat had the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Power?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, what do you call it? Administration--

speaker: interviewer
text: Control?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looked after it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Right. And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: So therefore, the few top people could go in and out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. So once you got the furniture into this is little flat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Ida stay--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after an aktion already because the flat was in a street which was, at the beginning, in the ghetto. And then, after the first aktion, when the ghetto was still smaller, this street became Aryan, became Polish because accommodation have been very shortened in Warsaw, especially after the bombings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So-- so sorry, excuse me. So this was in the smaller ghetto that became Aryan, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. After the aktions, when the ghetto was already-- the small ghetto was already disposed of, from the bigger ghetto, they also excluded a few streets because after the aktions, the population got smaller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you-- because you, of course, obviously, you had connections with Judenrat, did-- did the Judenrat become aware of-- at what point did they become aware of-- of the extermination camps? For example, after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --liquidation of Krakow, what was-- was there any news of this in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you please repeat this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. After the liquidation of the Krakow ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: What's Kra-ha?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow was not-- we'd been in Warsaw, not in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I know that, but-- but when the liquidation of the Krakow ghetto took place, was there any news of it in Warsaw? Did the Judenrat know of what-- at what point--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat knew, but not because of Krakow or because of Lodz, but because of what has been happening locally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there have been some Germans who did let you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. Yes. And so when the first aktion took place--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- were you there? Were you at the ghetto at the day of the aktion?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first-- I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the first one I've been in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what happened that day? Can you remember what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of people got taken to the Umschlagplatz and sent away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how were they called? Were they called into to the street? How were they selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came into the houses, and-- you come, you come, you come. And if you didn't want to, if there was a  little child-- I've seen it-- a little baby taken out of a cot and thrown against the wall because the mother didn't want to go. Anyway, they selected them. They came into the houses, and-- this house has to go. This house has to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people per day were taken? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it was said at that time that they are being transported to arbeitslager. That means work camps. And apparently, it was true. Part-- part of the people have been kept in, say, Lublin ghetto, which was, for quite a long time, a working place. But some, especially the old ones and the young ones, who have been not-- nonproductive, so to say, they have been straight into the ovens and to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was at Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Auschwitz and Treblinka. I remember Korczak. You may have heard about Dr. Korczak. That was the Pole--

speaker: interviewer
text: The children.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know him?

speaker: interviewer
text: With the children, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've seen him marching on top of the column of his children. He was offered to be exempted, but he didn't accept it. He went with his children, knowing that it is for death.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- and what-- what prompted, during-- what prompted the president of the Judenrat to commit suicide? What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam Czerniakow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he got to know that the exterminations are taking place. He wasn't told that it will be final because the final solution didn't-- has not been decreed till much later. But he got to know that the people who are being taken out apparently will be gassed. Anyway, after the first aktion, there came a couple of people, young blokes, back, because they wrapped themselves into the clothing which have been left by the people who went into the gas chambers. And the clothing has been loaded into trains. And when the train came to Warsaw, somehow or another, a couple of people came out and came back into the ghetto and told the Judenrat what's really happening and where the supposed work camps-- what really happens there. At that time, a lot of people wouldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What was the reaction to it? Disbelief or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Disbelief. The general-- general public, the general Jewish population wouldn't-- did not believe that it is possible that by the hundreds and by the thousands they are being not transported to work, but for extermination.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- too busy, really, to think much. I believed because I believed that the people who escaped are telling the truth. But it didn't really sink in. Don't forget that I've been very young and very, very busy arranging all sort of things for the Jews and for the Polish and keeping the connection with the resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what-- after the-- after-- after the suicide of the president, what-- what was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Licthenbaum, the vice president, became president.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the morale like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The general population didn't know what was happening. They lived from day to day. And the typhus was rife. You couldn't move in the street for the bodies lying in the streets and just covered with newspapers. And if it was a windy day, they would put a brick or a stone on top of it. The general population has been too busy to make a living to worry about what's really in the future-- what's happening-- what are the plans. And you lived from day to day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any movement for the formation of some sort of resistance to this particular situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, that is a very touchy questions because most people didn't know what is happening. But in fact, there have been. There have been two things going. I did get to know much later about it-- much later, not when it was formed. One was a very left-- leftish, even more than the Bund. Do you know about Bund? And did you know that there has been even a more to the left organization? And then there have been the revisionists and especially the one who have been in the Polish Army. And the two, they didn't see eye-to-eye.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reasons, Stephanie? Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had different plans for the ghetto. And here you are coming to a very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you switch off? Can you switch off for a minute? Stephanie, you were saying that your brother-in-law was part of the revisionist movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: From before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: From before the war. And there was an even more extreme left-wing movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish movement. And did the revisionists plan to coincide some sort of resistance with people from outside the ghetto? Or did they just plan--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is something I got to know after the war because during the war, when I've been-- because I had the right to go in and out, and I have been sent with a brown envelope to a certain address-- I didn't know what it was. I know it was given to me in the Judenrat. And I have been told to hand it over at the given address. But I did not know, really, what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: The contents. And so the revisionists planned a resistance--

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I got to know later.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in conjunction, yeah, with the Polish. And the-- the ultra left-wing people, what did they plan to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They planned to resist. But they did not take into account the loss of life of the ordinary person. All they wanted is to die with honor themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name--

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it came to the last aktion, to the final one, raving, hopping, skipping from one bunker to the other one, giving away the location of the bunkers to the Germans because they had to have some food. [EXHALES] That's also a well-known thing today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the-- was there a name for this left-wing group, the Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no-- no name at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was Aniewlewicz who has headed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they have made, for example, money by kidnapping people and demanding ransom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kidnapping who?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not really a very honorable thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Kidnapping whom? People from within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who had-- who have been known to have money and from the Judenrat itself. For example, the young Lichtenbaum, the engineer, because he was the son of the president, he was also kidnapped. And the Judenrat has paid something to have him back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with the revisionists' resistance, did that take the form of food storage and places to hide or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't been in-- in the know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how-- how did they plan to resist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: The revisionists.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the war, I did get to know that they wanted to join the Polish resistance whenever the rebellion will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so just to clarify, the Polish army, Polish-- remnants of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Home Army. The Home Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Home Army was going to launch a resistance against the Germans outside the ghetto. And the people within the ghetto would join that resistance. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only sort of, not exactly. Far, not exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- in the period between the demise of the ghetto and-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: There have been three aktions.

speaker: interviewer
text: After, yes--

speaker: interviewee
text: The demise didn't happen suddenly.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. And after the-- after the-- after the third aktion, which I-- I think-- I think it took place September-- I think something like--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the last one was in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- that's of-- of 1943. But the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when that-- between the-- end of 1942--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a first, second, and third one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Third, yeah. Between the end of 1942 and this April aktion--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- were you just-- were you still just working for the building department and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was time when Vera's husband was assassinated. We came back from our flat from the Polish side one night, one evening. He was between Vera and myself. We walked all three, per arm. Is that what to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. And a few steps from the entrance to our house, he's got shot in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: And fell down, immediately dead. Dead. Through the heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, I just-- I have to clarify. When you say you came back from your flat in-- on the Aryan side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vera was coming in to see her husband, but actually living on the Aryan side at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Vera was, really, still living in the ghetto because her husband and I, we have been moving freely. But she didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So how was-- she had been to visit her children or something. Is this correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she move?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: She used your pass. Right. And what was the name of your pass? What was its official name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. OK. And that was issued to you by the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or by German high command?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the German SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German SS. Right. And you had to show that on demand?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the gates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes. And whatever-- and did you-- going in and out of the ghetto, did you personally experience any violence towards you or any-- what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I experienced some theft, so to say, because I smuggled in all sort of little things for the kids-- food, butter, this sort of things. And sometimes, when the SS man was in a bad mood and found it, then it did cost me a watch or whatever. But I had other ways of smuggling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were moving freely, you-- you-- you would have been, I guess, a very obvious target for being searched, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: For?

speaker: interviewer
text: Being searched as-- as all of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Searched? Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --as you came in your house, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mostly went by car. I came back on foot. But I went out by car with the engineers to the building site. The engineers in-- stayed in the building site. I have been there, too, and gave the workers some money or whatever-- official. And then I disappeared [LAUGHS] and came back much later by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you were not on-- like, when you were by yourself in Warsaw? What-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my Polish resistance friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing at that time? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the male friends of mine, they have been going to all sort of exercises, Home Army. But because I've been Jewish, they feared that I might be give-- giving away things. So I have been assigned, because of the good knowledge of German, to report all sort of things like movements of trains and this sort of things-- movements of supplies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you said you had other ways of smuggling things in and out of the ghetto, what were those ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, there have been other people who also planned and had friends on the other side and wanted to smuggle out some money or things. For example, I had, in my back teeth, the biggest molars-- drilled out and put on just cheap, little crowns, not gold ones. And underneath, I smuggled diamonds. Or in winter, in the very end of the glove, I smuggled [LAUGHS] rings and other things out. And when I brought the Kennkarten, the Polish-- for the-- when somebody wanted to go onto the Polish side and ask for false paper--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what the-- Could you tell us what the Kennkarten is about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the Germans came, after the siege of Warsaw, they have ordered a-- what do you call it? When you register everybody-- what is it called, for goodness sake? Every four years, you have it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: A census.

speaker: interviewee
text: A census. That's right. [LAUGHS] Now, they ordered a census but with it, also, the issuing of cards, of identity cards. The Kennkarte is really, in English, an identity card with a photograph and with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stamp, finger--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fingerprint.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fingerprint. Well, that's it. Everybody, every Pole, or every person living on the Polish side-- because there have been the ethnic Germans, as well-- had to have it. They had to front up in their office like you have here, electoral office, they have been offices. You had to front up, and they've been issued. You had to bring your birth certificate. And you got issued with the card.

speaker: interviewer
text: And therefore, you were bringing in-- were these-- were these false Kennkarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family-- everybody, including Vera's husband, first, Israel. Vera-- no, Vera didn't go. Vera was too frightened. But [INAUDIBLE] grandmother with two kids and I, we fronted up and got a false birth certificate, of course, presented it, and got the real identity cards issued by the Germans, which-- but in a new name, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which name did you have the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bogacka, which means in Polish the same as rich, translated, simply translated. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say you were-- you brought Kennkarten into the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these false ones? Or were these--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was false ones from the resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they must have been very precious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that depends. If somebody could pay for it, they asked for it. If absolutely impossible, if it was a very, very poor person-- he got it without.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the value of the Kennkarten for someone in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- what was the benefit? What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That if they have finally decided to leave the ghetto, if they had a  means or somebody who would help them, then they could go onto the Polish side and live in hiding. For example, after the first-- or was it the second aktion? The cashier of the Judenrat by the name of Melamed, he lost his wife. Oh, I thought for a minute I kicked you. [LAUGHS] He lost his wife. And after he lost his wife, he has decided to leave the Judenrat and leave the ghetto. I've brought him a Kennkarte. He contacted his friends from before the war in Praga, in the same suburb where we lived before the war. And there was a little shopkeeper who had a cellar under his shop. And this Melamed, the cashier, they hidden him there. And I visited him there a few times. And the one time where I came they told me, go away quickly because he has given himself up to the Germans. He couldn't stand anymore living in the hiding all the time. So I have been told to disappear quickly because they may be under observation. And that happened, too, that people did get a good hiding place. And they couldn't stand that. But there have been other people who did go out on the cards which I brought in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you-- were you at all afraid that you would be found with all these Kennkarten on your--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --person coming back into the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what is being afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still don't know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephanie, did you think that was because you were so young or because you-- just the moment of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I have been determined that I will make it, absolutely determined that I will make it. Just as well as, much later, here, in Australia, I also had some reasons. And I still believe I will make it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what happened to you when-- when-- can you tell us what happened when the final aktion took place in April? Do you remember what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the final aktion took place in April, I've been that day in the council's building, in the building department. And the Germans came in and ordered all, with the exception of a few people, to get out of the building. And I've been among the ones who've been allowed to stay because they needed still some paperwork. As I stood at the window of the first floor, and I've seen a German SS man throwing a grenade into the cellars-- you know, in Warsaw, the houses have been built differently than here. The multi-story houses had all cellars. And people have been bricking over the windows from the cellars and made it-- making it into little bunkers. And I've seen one of the Germans throwing a grenade into such a little bunker and people climbing out there. And I can still remember as-- as if I've seen it, a boy about six, seven, maybe eight years coming out of this with his hair on fire, the face half burned off, hair's on fire. And he still walked out. And behind him, other people walking out of the bunker. And this I've seen from the window of the first floor. And they marched all the people to waiting trucks. Some, who wouldn't go quickly enough, they got shot straightaway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, some of us have been kept back. And when the work was finished, we've been taken on a truck and put into the SS and Gestapo building in Zelazna Street. That was really the headquarters of the SS administration of the ghetto. That's what it really was. They've put us there in a cellar. And we've been there for, oh, three or four nights. And we got fed a little bit. And when the final direction comes that the ghetto will not have any more, not even a small number, of people left, that it is a final solution-- because of the resistance, they have decided to finish it off altogether, quickly. We've been taken to the Polish jail, to the Pawiak, very well known. And towards the end of the day, some more people have been loaded on the truck and taken to Umshclag, Umshclagplatz. That was a railway, end of the railway, which, taking the wagons, the trains, to the different camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can I just ask you, at this stage, the actual ghetto uprising was taking place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. So you were at the SS headquarters-- or administrative headquarters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Down, under.

speaker: interviewer
text: Downstairs. And you knew about the resistance in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that the resistance in the ghetto was taking place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've seen it from the windows because-- during the first day.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then, you were taken with this band of people to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Umschlagplatz or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were loaded into a train. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've-- in the same compartment, the same wagon, or carriage, or whatever you call it-- it was a cattle train. And 100 or 120 people have been crowded into it. You could hardly place-- hardly a place to stand. But some people have been determined that they won't make it and gave up all hope. But other young people didn't think like this. And especially young boys came to the side of the wagon. The wagon was in the shape of a rectangle-- long, long and short across. And there wasn't-- haven't been windows. But there have been tiny, little holes, just the width of one board. And two of the strong young men, or boys, or whatever you want to call them, have managed to push out one more bit of the next board. And whoever wanted to jump, take the risk of jumping, came, squeezed to this little opening.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll have to stop there. Sorry. Stephanie, we'll stop. We'll put this on the next tape.
